When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.content: Looking at the image, the white strip moved LEFT and is now back near the left part of the blue bar. Let me check the exact positions to see if the left blue bar part was absorbed.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state after LEFT
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    if n['color'] not in ['w', 'g']:
        print(f"id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, bbox=({min(rows)},{min(cols)})-({max(rows)},{max(cols)}), children={n['children']}")

# Count blue objects
blue_objs = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'b']
print(f"\nBlue objects: {len(blue_objs)}")
for n in blue_objs:
    print(f"  pixels={n['pixels']}, bbox=({min(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])},{min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])})-({max(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])},{max(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK so after moving LEFT:
- White strip at (28,28)-(32,31) with blue center at (29,29)-(30,30)
- Blue bar at (28,32)-(31,39) with 18 pixels (the right part)
- The left blue bar part (10 pixels) is GONE! It was absorbed by the white strip.
So the white strip absorbed the left blue bar part when it moved LEFT. Now the blue bar is back to being a single piece at (28,32)-(31,39) with 18 pixels.
Wait, but earlier the blue bar was split into two parts: left (10 pixels) and right (10 pixels). After moving LEFT, the white strip absorbed the left part, leaving only the right part (18 pixels). But 10 + 10 = 20, and now we have 18 pixels. So 2 pixels were lost or merged.
Actually, looking more carefully, the blue bar at (28,32)-(31,39) has 18 pixels. This is the same as before when the blue bar was at (28,32)-(31,39) with 18 pixels (after the first collision). So the white strip absorbed the left part (10 pixels) and the right part is still 18 pixels. But wait, the right part was 10 pixels before, not 18. Let me re-examine.
Actually, I think I'm confusing the states. Let me trace the blue bar:
1. Initial: blue bar at (28,28)-(31,39) with 28 pixels (4x12 hollow rectangle)
2. After first UP (white strip collides with blue bar): blue bar split, left part absorbed, remaining blue bar at (28,32)-(31,39) with 18 pixels
3. After RIGHT (white strip moves into middle): blue bar split into left (10 pixels) and right (10 pixels)
4. After LEFT (white strip moves left): left blue bar part absorbed, remaining blue bar at (28,32)-(31,39) with 18 pixels
Wait, that doesn't add up. In step 2, the remaining blue bar has 18 pixels. In step 3, the blue bar is split into two 10-pixel parts (total 20 pixels). In step 4, the remaining blue bar has 18 pixels again.
Hmm, maybe the blue bar is regenerating or changing shape. Or maybe I'm miscounting.
Let me just focus on the current state and figure out what to do next.
Current state:
- White strip at (28,28)-(32,31) with blue center
- Blue bar at (28,32)-(31,39) with 18 pixels
- Player at (33,28)-(35,31)
- 2 charcoal squares at (24,44) and (28,16)
The white strip is adjacent to the blue bar (white strip right edge at col 31, blue bar left edge at col 32). If I move RIGHT, the white strip will move into the blue bar and absorb part of it.
Let me try moving RIGHT again to absorb more of the blue bar.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current state:
# - White strip at (28,28)-(32,31)
# - Blue bar at (28,32)-(31,39) with 18 pixels
# - Player at (33,28)-(35,31)
#
# White strip is adjacent to blue bar (cols 31 vs 32)
# Moving RIGHT will move white strip into blue bar
# Let me try RIGHT to absorb more of the blue bar
action(['RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
